query: intracellular iron homeostasis mechanisms in iron deficiency anemia

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Iron Deficiency -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Iron enters cells after transferrin binds to the transferrin receptor (TfR). Iron levels are further controlled by iron-regulatory proteins 1 and 2(IRP1 and IRP2), which optimize cellular iron availability by upregulating the expression of multiple genes. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Iron Supplementation -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Iron is a critical component of the body. Its primary role is to store and transport iron (as myoglobin and hemoglobin) throughout the body. In an iron-deficient state, hemoglobin cannot be synthesized, with resultant microcytic anemia due to the formation of small erythrocytes.

retrieved_docs.title: Iron Supplementation -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Iron is a medication used in the management and treatment of iron deficiency anemia. This activity illustrates the indications, action, and contraindications for iron supplementation as a valuable agent in the management of iron-deficient states such as iron deficiency anemia, iron deficiency without anemia, nutritional deficiency, malabsorption, blood loss, or an increase in the body's need for iron. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors (e.g., off-label uses, dosing, pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics, monitoring, relevant interactions) pertinent for members of the healthcare team in the management of patients with iron deficiency and related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Iron Overload and Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Recent advances in iron metabolism have further defined the central regulatory role of hepcidin in systemic iron balance and its dysregulation in both iron overload and iron deficiency disorders. [23] Increasing evidence has linked iron accumulation to hepatic and endocrine dysfunction and neurodegenerative diseases, highlighting the systemic consequences of iron dysregulation. Identifying ferroptosis—a distinct, iron-dependent form of regulated cell death—has expanded the current understanding of iron-mediated cytotoxicity. [24] [25] Ferritinophagy, the autophagic degradation of ferritin, has also been recognized as a critical mechanism in intracellular iron homeostasis and oxidative stress responses. These mechanistic insights have prompted the development of targeted therapies, including agents that modulate the hepcidin-ferroportin axis and novel iron chelators designed to mitigate iron-induced tissue damage. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Iron -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Iron is a mineral essential to the human body. It is administered for the treatment of iron deficiency. It is available in many formulations that belong to the class of iron products. This activity outlines the mechanism of action, indications, contraindications, relevant monitoring, and risk of toxicity. Knowledge of this topic is essential for members of an interprofessional team to provide proper care to patients with iron deficiency and related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Iron Overload and Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Iron homeostasis in humans is primarily maintained by regulating intestinal iron absorption. Normal plasma iron concentrations range from 12 to 25 μmol/L and are sustained through a balance between dietary iron absorbed by enterocytes and iron recycled from senescent erythrocytes by macrophages. Ferroportin, the only known cellular iron exporter, facilitates iron efflux from enterocytes and macrophages into plasma. Hepcidin, a liver-derived peptide hormone, tightly regulates ferroportin activity. Increased hepcidin concentrations induce ferroportin internalization and degradation, thereby decreasing plasma iron levels. In contrast, suppressed hepcidin expression permits sustained ferroportin activity and increases plasma iron concentrations. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Iron -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the mechanism of action of iron therapy. Describe the adverse effects of iron therapy. Review the appropriate monitoring for toxicity of iron therapy. Discuss interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance iron therapy successfully and safely and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion Iron Overload -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: One unit of transfused blood contains approximately 200 to 250 mg of iron. [3] Patients who undergo more than 10 to 20 units of blood transfusions face a substantial risk of developing iron overload. [3] No effective physiological mechanism for removing excess iron from the human body exists. For this reason, the body requires tight regulation of iron absorption and recycling.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Iron Deficiency -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Iron is essential for multiple biological functions of the body. It is necessary for the synthesis of hemoglobin, myoglobin, cell regulation/proliferation, DNA synthesis, and electron transport in the mitochondria. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Iron -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Of note, these processes of active absorption can only absorb a set amount of iron at a given time. Iron consumed exceeding these limits is absorbed passively into the blood via the paracellular route. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Iron Deficiency -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The maintenance of iron homeostasis occurs via a balance of absorption and iron losses. Absorption in the small intestine is the primary regulating factor of iron content in the body. Iron is absorbed in divalent form by enterocytes via the divalent metal transporter (DMT1). Once absorbed, it is sequestered in ferritin if body stores are adequate. If iron levels are low, iron is bound to transferrin by ferroportin. Once loaded on to transferrin, iron is transported to cells for metabolism. Hepcidin is a peptide that inhibits ferroportin and decreases the absorption of iron into circulation. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Deferoxamine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Iron is an essential part of human physiology. It is a vital element in proteins such as hemoglobin, myoglobin, and cytochrome and also functions as a cofactor for many enzymes. It is stored by ferritin and transferred throughout the serum by transferrin. No physiological mechanism exists to excrete iron. Instead, humans regulate GI uptake by altering hepcidin levels. [15] When iron storing proteins become saturated, free iron species accumulate in the plasma. These include non-transferable bound iron (NTBI) and labile plasma iron (LBI). [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Normochromic Normocytic Anemia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Increase in Pro-inflammatory Cytokines and Iron Dysregulation: This increase is associated with a variety of disease states, including infection, neoplasm, chronic kidney disease, as well as autoimmune conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis and systemic lupus erythematosus. Hepcidin is the main modulator of iron metabolism, which is key in chronic disease and anemia pathophysiology. Interleukin-6 (IL-6) and other proinflammatory cytokines facilitate an acute-phase protein, hepcidin upregulation. Hepcidin binds to the iron export protein, ferroportin, in macrophages, hepatocytes, and enterocytes. Increased hepcidin levels lead to iron trapping within macrophages and hepatocytes, resulting in low circulating iron levels. Through negative feedback stimulated by increased iron stores, hepcidin causes enterocytes and macrophages to degrade ferroportin, thus reducing absorption and promoting storage. [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Anemia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia of chronic disease is a common form of anemia seen in hospitalized patients caused by long-standing diseases, infections, inflammations, and malignancies. These chronic conditions cause increased production of hepcidin [5] by the liver. Hepcidin binds to its receptor ferroportin and decreases intestinal iron absorption and decreases the release of iron from liver and macrophages, leading to decreased availability of iron for erythropoiesis. Other mechanisms that contribute to anemia are by inflammatory cytokines that increase red cell destruction, suppress the proliferation of erythroid precursors, and by inhibiting the release of erythropoietin from kidneys.

retrieved_docs.title: Sideroblastic Anemia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: A mutation of HSPA9 , the mitochondrial chaperone protein, causes SIDBA4. As in previous types, various mutations occur, including missense, nonsense, frameshift, and in-frame deletion mutations. The absence of HSPA9 in erythroid cell lines inhibits erythroid cell differentiation.

retrieved_docs.title: Iron -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Oral iron – Iron administered orally travels to the duodenum, where it is taken up into enterocyte cells along the brush border by the divalent metal-ion transporter 1 (DMT1). From there, it can either be incorporated into ferritin for storage or expelled into the serum by the ferroportin 1 transporter on the basolateral membrane. This transporter is bound to multicopper oxidases that oxidize ferrous iron (Fe2+) into ferric iron (Fe3+) that can bind to transferrin for transport to the bone marrow for hemoglobin synthesis or to the liver for storage. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1912
retrieved_docs.content: Ferric maltol and sodium ironedetate are both members of the therapeutic class identified as iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations. This class comprises oral formulations that deliver trivalent iron to patients with iron‑deficiency anemia, thereby replenishing depleted iron stores and supporting hemoglobin synthesis. By belonging to the same category, ferric maltol and sodium ironedetate share a common mechanism of action and are used interchangeably as oral options for correcting iron deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1444
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified as an “Other deficiency anemia.” The category of Other deficiency anemias includes folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات), pernicious anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات). Recognizing that anemia in chronic kidney disease belongs to this broader group highlights shared pathogenic mechanisms among these deficiency‑related anemias and guides clinicians toward comprehensive evaluation and management strategies that address the underlying nutrient deficiencies.

By incorporating folic acid into multivitamin‑iron supplements, clinicians aim to correct or avert folate deficiency, support normal erythropoiesis, and improve overall hematologic health while simultaneously providing other essential vitamins (A, C, D, E, B‑complex) and minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, iodine) that contribute to broader metabolic and physiological functions.

Collectively, these relationships illustrate how specific oral iron preparations—whether bivalent iron‑ascorbate salts or broader multivitamin‑iron combinations—are organized within therapeutic classes and formulated to maximize iron bioavailability while addressing concurrent micronutrient deficiencies commonly seen in patients with iron deficiency anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1384
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified as a form of “Other deficiency anemias.” The category of Other deficiency anemias encompasses several distinct types, including فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات (folate‑deficiency anemia), فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات (iron‑deficiency anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية (autoimmune hemolytic anemia), and فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (non‑immune hemolytic anemia). Recognizing that anemia associated with chronic kidney disease falls within this broader group helps clinicians consider the underlying nutritional or hemolytic mechanisms that may contribute to the patient’s low hemoglobin and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and targeted treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1237
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is categorized under the broader group of Other deficiency anemias. The category Other deficiency anemias includes specific types such as folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات) and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات). It also has subordinate subtypes, including non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) and acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب). Recognizing that anemia associated with chronic kidney disease belongs to this classification helps clinicians consider overlapping mechanisms—such as nutrient deficiencies and impaired erythropoiesis—when evaluating and managing patients.

Collectively, these multivitamin‑iron and zinc products aim to improve hematopoiesis, correct simultaneous micronutrient deficiencies, enhance iron absorption (often facilitated by vitamin C and chelated iron forms), reduce gastrointestinal side effects, and provide comprehensive metabolic support for patients at risk of iron‑deficiency anemia, folate‑deficiency anemia, combined nutritional deficits, or those requiring bariatric supplementation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1391
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified as an “Other deficiency anemia,” placing it within a broader category of deficiency‑related blood disorders. The “Other deficiency anemias” group includes folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات), iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات), and a non‑regenerative form of anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) as a subcategory. It also encompasses hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmune mechanisms (فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية). By situating anemia in chronic kidney disease among these specific deficiency and hemolytic conditions, clinicians can appreciate the diverse etiologic pathways—nutrient deficiencies and non‑autoimmune hemolysis—that may contribute to anemia in this patient population, guiding more comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 5 -- Community 2929
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymology, the scientific study of enzymes and their biochemical actions, is recognized as a relevant qualifier for several distinct medical topics. In the context of Cannabis, enzymology helps elucidate how the plant’s cannabinoids are metabolized, how enzyme‑mediated pathways influence its pharmacodynamics, and how enzymatic interactions may affect both therapeutic benefits and adverse effects. Enzymology also provides essential insight into فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد (iron‑deficiency anemia), where enzyme‑dependent processes such as heme synthesis, iron transport, and erythropoiesis are central to the disorder’s pathophysiology and to the development of targeted diagnostic and therapeutic strategies. Additionally, enzymology is pertinent to gout and other crystal arthropathies, because enzyme‑driven metabolic pathways—particularly those governing purine turnover, uric acid production, and crystal formation—underlie the onset and progression of these conditions. By integrating enzymological knowledge across Cannabis use, iron‑deficiency anemia, and gout‑related crystal arthropathies, clinicians and researchers can achieve a more comprehensive understanding of disease mechanisms, improve diagnostic accuracy, and tailor interventions that consider the underlying enzymatic processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 711
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified among other deficiency anemias. The category of other deficiency anemias includes folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات), pernicious anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات). Additionally, other deficiency anemias are related to cold‑type hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد). Recognizing that anemia in chronic kidney disease belongs to this broader group helps clinicians consider overlapping pathophysiological mechanisms and select appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039
retrieved_docs.content: Iron in other combinations is classified as an iron antianemic preparation. Iron antianemic preparations are therapeutic agents used to replenish iron stores and treat iron‑deficiency anemia. Formulations described as “iron in other combinations” contain iron together with additional compounds or nutrients, enhancing absorption or providing complementary benefits while still serving the primary purpose of correcting iron deficiency. Recognizing these combination products as a subset of iron antianemic preparations helps clinicians select appropriate therapy for patients with anemia, especially when co‑existing deficiencies or specific tolerability issues are present.

It also includes acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب) as a child category, as well as non‑immune hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity (فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية). Recognizing that anemia in chronic kidney disease belongs to this heterogeneous group helps clinicians consider underlying nutritional deficiencies and hemolytic mechanisms when evaluating and managing affected patients.

Calcium, essential fatty acids and probiotics contribute to metabolic health, gut microbiota balance and bowel regularity. The integrated ingredient profile of the IRON/MULTIVATAMINS family reflects a comprehensive strategy to deliver iron together with complementary vitamins and minerals, addressing the heightened micronutrient requirements after surgery, especially bariatric procedures, and promoting optimal patient nutrition, recovery and overall health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 427
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified under the broader category of Other deficiency anemias. Other deficiency anemias include folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات), megaloblastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات), thalassemia (الثلاسيميا), and non‑immune hemolytic anemia (فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية). Thalassemia is clinically associated with sickle‑cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية). Recognizing that anemia in chronic kidney disease belongs to this spectrum helps clinicians address the specific deficiency mechanisms and related complications, such as retinal disease in patients with thalassemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 147
retrieved_docs.content: while iron (as ferrous fumarate, ferrous asparto‑glycinate, polysaccharide iron complex, iron sucrose, ferric pyrophosphate, iron ascorbate, ferro‑cysteine glycine, or ferrous amino‑acid chelate) addresses iron‑deficiency anemia. The chelated form (FERROUS AMINO ACID CHELATE) exemplifies the “Iron in combination with folic acid” sub‑category, enhancing iron absorption when paired with folic acid. Additional vitamins and minerals—vitamin B‑complex, vitamin C, vitamin A, vitamin D, vitamin K, vitamin B12, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, and essential fatty acids—provide synergistic support for hematopoiesis, red‑cell formation, and overall metabolic health. Collectively, these iron‑containing multivitamin and antianemic products integrate iron, folic acid, and a spectrum of micronutrients to correct anemia, prevent macro‑ and micro‑nutrient deficiencies, and promote comprehensive patient nutritional status.
